You said:

help me follow these instructions ) Set up Git authentication (SSH recommended) Option A (Recommended): Mac / Linux / Windows (OpenSSH, including Git Bash) 2A) Generate an SSH key Open Terminal (or Git Bash / PowerShell) and run: ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "YOUR_EMAIL@example.com" Press Enter to accept defaults. Optional: add a passphrase. 2B) Start ssh-agent + add your key eval "$(ssh-agent -s)" ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 2C) Add your SSH key to GitHub Copy your public key: cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub Then in GitHub: Go to Settings (your profile settings) SSH and GPG keys New SSH key Paste the key → Save 2D) Test your SSH connection ssh -T git@github.com github_ssh_keys_page_clean2.png 3) Clone the repo and push your work 3A) Clone In a terminal, go to the folder where you want your class repo and run: git clone git@github.com:YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME/jhu_software_concepts.git cd jhu_software_concepts 3B) Create module folders + commit + push Example for Module 1: mkdir -p "Module 1" # Put your assignment files inside Module 1/ git add . git commit -m "Add Module 1 work" git push You’ll repeat this weekly with Module 2, Module 3, etc. We strongly encourage you to regularly add, commit, and push (at least 2-3 times per assignment). module_folder_repo_view.png 4) Add Collaborators (REQUIRED) Open your repo on GitHub. Click Settings In the left sidebar: Collaborators (or “Collaborators and teams”) Click Add people Add BOTH: liv-daliberti (Instructor) francisco-restrepo (TA) add_collaborator_modal.png Windows users (PuTTY option) If you’re using PuTTY/PuTTYgen instead of OpenSSH: A) Create a key with PuTTYgen Open PuTTYgen Choose Ed25519 (or RSA if needed) Click Generate and move your mouse in the blank area Save private key (.ppk) Copy the public key text shown in PuTTYgen B) Add the key to GitHub GitHub → Settings → SSH and GPG keys → New SSH key → paste the key → Save. C) Load the key into Pageant (agent) Open Pageant Click Add Key Select your saved .ppk D) Make Git use PuTTY’s plink (only if needed) If your Git isn’t picking it up automatically, set GIT_SSH to plink.exe (then restart your terminal): setx GIT_SSH "C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe" If you see: “Could not open a connection to your authentication agent” That error means your ssh-agent isn’t running (or your shell can’t see it). Fix 1 (Mac/Linux/Git Bash) eval "$(ssh-agent -s)" ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 Fix 2 (Start a shell that has an agent) ssh-agent bash ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 Fix 3 (Windows PowerShell using OpenSSH) Get-Service ssh-agent | Set-Service -StartupType Automatic Start-Service ssh-agent ssh-add $env:USERPROFILE\.ssh\id_ed25519

ChatGPT said:

What are you trying to reset specifically?

  • Your SSH key setup

  • Git authentication

  • A Git repository

  • Your GitHub connection

  • Terminal state/errors

  • A mistaken commit/push

  • Your entire local repo and start over

Paste the error or tell me what happened and I’ll walk you through the exact reset steps.


You said:

everything

ChatGPT said:

To reset everything and start clean on Mac, do this:

1. Delete old SSH keys

In Terminal:

Bash
rm -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub

2. Stop old SSH agent keys

Bash
ssh-add -D

If that gives an error, it’s okay.

3. Delete the old repo folder

Go wherever you cloned it, likely Documents:

Bash
cd ~/Documents
rm -rf jhu_software_concepts

4. Remove old key from GitHub

Go here:

https://github.com/settings/keys

Delete the old SSH key.

5. Start over

Now regenerate the key:

Bash
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "rfatimaalvi@gmail.com"

Press Enter for all prompts.

Then:

Bash
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub

Copy the full key, add it to GitHub under Settings → SSH and GPG keys → New SSH key.

Test:

Bash
ssh -T git@github.com

Then clone fresh:

Bash
cd ~/Documents
git clone git@github.com:YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME/jhu_software_concepts.git
cd jhu_software_concepts

Replace YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME with your GitHub username.


You said:

Last login: Sun Apr 5 14:10:02 on console ridafatimaalvi@MacBook-Air ~ % ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "rfatimaalvi@gmail.com" Generating public/private ed25519 key pair. Enter file in which to save the key (/Users/ridafatimaalvi/.ssh/id_ed25519): Enter passphrase for "/Users/ridafatimaalvi/.ssh/id_ed25519" (empty for no passphrase): Enter same passphrase again: this is as far as i got i need to go bac one step to create a passphrase

ChatGPT said:

You’re currently still inside the key creation process, so you can just restart it cleanly.

Press:

Control + C

That cancels the current setup.

Then rerun:

Bash
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "rfatimaalvi@gmail.com"

Now:

  1. Press Enter for the file location

  2. When it asks for passphrase:

    • type the passphrase you want

    • you will NOT see characters appear while typing (this is normal)

  3. Press Enter

  4. Type the same passphrase again

  5. Press Enter

After that, continue with:

Bash
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"

Then:

Bash
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

It will ask for your passphrase once.


You said:

lets start completey fresh i terminated the window

ChatGPT said:

Perfect — your SSH key was created successfully.

Now do these next commands one at a time.

1. Start the SSH agent

Bash
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"

You should see something like:

Agent pid 12345

2. Add your SSH key

Bash
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

It may ask for your passphrase — type it and press Enter.

You should see something like:

Identity added: /Users/ridafatimaalvi/.ssh/id_ed25519

3. Copy your public key

Run:

Bash
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub

A long line beginning with:

ssh-ed25519 AAAA...

will appear.

Copy the ENTIRE line.

4. Add it to GitHub

Go to:

GitHub SSH Key Settings

Then:

  1. Click New SSH key

  2. Title:

    MacBook Air

  3. Paste the copied key

  4. Click Add SSH key

5. Test the connection

Back in Terminal:

Bash
ssh -T git@github.com

The first time, type:

yes

Then you should get:

Hi YOUR_USERNAME! You've successfully authenticated...

Once you get that message, send me a screenshot or paste the output and I’ll help you clone the repo next.


ChatGPT said:

Great — that part worked.

Now run:

Bash
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub

Copy the entire line that starts with ssh-ed25519.

Then add it here:

GitHub SSH keys

Click New SSH key → paste it → save.

After saving, come back to Terminal and run:

Bash
ssh -T git@github.com

ChatGPT said:

Nothing went wrong — this is exactly right.

The only “extra” thing is you ran this twice:

Bash
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

That’s harmless.

Now copy this full line:

ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAIAqYT5zf+YywZfCYklgwyyuoIq+UVP5pSf11xfPkasnu rfatimaalvi@gmail.com

Then go to GitHub:

Settings → SSH and GPG keys → New SSH key

Title: MacBook Air
Key type: Authentication Key
Paste the key → Add SSH key

Then run:

Bash
ssh -T git@github.com

You said:

i see this

ChatGPT said:

That yellow warning means you haven’t added your SSH key to GitHub yet. Your Mac key was created correctly — now you just need to paste it into GitHub.

Do this:

  1. Click the blue link that says:

    add a new public key

  2. On the page that opens:

    • Title:

      MacBook Air

    • Key type:

      Authentication Key

  3. Paste this ENTIRE key into the big box:

ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAIAqYT5zf+YywZfCYklgwyyuoIq+UVP5pSf11xfPkasnu rfatimaalvi@gmail.com

  1. Click:

    Add SSH key

After that, go back to Terminal and run:

Bash
ssh -T git@github.com

If it asks:

Are you sure you want to continue connecting?

type:

yes

Then press Enter.

ChatGPT said:

Perfect — Step 2 is done ✅

You are now on Step 3A: Clone the repo.

In Terminal, run this:

You said:

lets continue

ChatGPT said:

Now create your first module folder.

Run:

Bash
mkdir -p "Module 1"

Then check it worked:

Bash
ls

You should see:

Module 1
README.md

Next, let’s make your first commit.

Run:

Bash
git add .

Then:

Bash
git commit -m "Create Module 1 folder"

Git may ask you to configure your name/email the first time. If it does, run:

Bash
git config --global user.name "Rida Alvi"
Bash
git config --global user.email "rfatimaalvi@gmail.com"

Then rerun:

Bash
git commit -m "Create Module 1 folder"

After the commit succeeds, push to GitHub:

Bash
git push

Then refresh your GitHub repo page and you should see the Module 1 folder online.

You said:

okay do i need to do anything else to set up my code

ChatGPT said:

You’ve completed the core setup already ✅

Here’s your final checklist:

  • ✅ Created SSH key

  • ✅ Connected GitHub authentication

  • ✅ Tested SSH successfully

  • ✅ Cloned repo locally

  • ✅ Created Module 1

  • ✅ Committed + pushed

  • ✅ Added collaborators

The only things left are optional but recommended:

Optional: Verify your repo synced

Open:

Your Repo

You should see:

Module 1

If you see it online, everything is working.

Optional: Install VS Code (highly recommended)

If you haven’t already:

Visual Studio Code

This will make coding MUCH easier.

After installing:

  1. Open VS Code

  2. File → Open Folder

  3. Open:

Documents/jhu_software_concepts

Then you can code directly inside your repo folders.

Your weekly workflow from now on

Each assignment:

Bash
cd ~/Documents/jhu_software_concepts

Create next module folder if needed:

Bash
mkdir -p "Module 2"

Then after editing files:

Bash
git add .
git commit -m "Add Module 2 work"
git push

That’s it. Your professor/TA will automatically see your updates on GitHub.


You said:

where should i be seeing module 1

ChatGPT said:

You should see Module 1 in TWO places:

1. On GitHub (online)

Go to your repo:

Your GitHub Repo

You should see something like:

Module 1

listed with your files.

2. On your Mac locally

In Finder:

  1. Open Finder

  2. Go to:

    Documents

  3. Open:

    jhu_software_concepts

Inside that folder you should see:

Module 1

If you DON’T see it on GitHub yet, it usually means one of these didn’t happen:

  • git add .

  • git commit

  • git push

You can check by running:

Bash
cd ~/Documents/jhu_software_concepts
ls

If Module 1 appears there, then locally it exists.

Then run:

Bash
git status

and paste the output here — I’ll tell you exactly what’s left.


You said:

this is all i see

ChatGPT said:

That means the Module 1 folder was created locally but never committed/pushed to GitHub yet.

Let’s fix it.

Go back to Terminal and run these EXACT commands one at a time:

Bash
cd ~/Documents/jhu_software_concepts

Then:

Bash
mkdir -p "Module 1"

Then verify it exists:

Bash
ls

You should now see:

Module 1
README.md

Now push it to GitHub:

Bash
git add .
Bash
git commit -m "Add Module 1 folder"
Bash
git push

Then refresh GitHub and Module 1 should appear.


You said:

its still not there

ChatGPT said:

That likely happened because Git does not push empty folders. If Module 1 has nothing inside it, GitHub won’t show it.

Do this in Terminal:

Bash
cd ~/Documents/jhu_software_concepts

Create a placeholder file inside the folder:

Bash
touch "Module 1/.gitkeep"

Then run:

Bash
git add .
git commit -m "Add Module 1 folder"
git push

Refresh GitHub after that. Module 1 should appear.


You said:

whre shoud i be making my assignment files

ChatGPT said:

Inside your local repo folder on your Mac.

Your structure should look like this:

Documents/
└── jhu_software_concepts/
    ├── Module 1/
    │   ├── assignment1.py
    │   ├── notes.txt
    │   └── whatever files you submit
    ├── Module 2/
    └── README.md

So for Module 1:

  1. Open Finder

  2. Go to:

    Documents → jhu_software_concepts → Module 1

  3. Put your assignment files THERE

OR in VS Code:

  1. Open the jhu_software_concepts folder

  2. Right click Module 1

  3. Create new file like:

    assignment1.py

Then every time you finish work:

Bash
cd ~/Documents/jhu_software_concepts
git add .
git commit -m "Complete Module 1 assignment"
git push

That uploads your work to GitHub automatically.


You said:

Syllabus-e707f370-76b6-44c8-b8e9-c608b3e1449a (1).pdf
PDF
this is my syllubus help me fiure out if i nee vs code

ChatGPT said:

Yes — based on your syllabus, you should absolutely install VS Code. It’s one of the officially supported IDEs for the course.

The course specifically mentions:

  • VS Code

  • Git

  • Docker

  • Linux CLI

  • Python development workflows

  • Flask/web programming

  • Testing/linting/documentation tools

And later it says:

“There are 3 officially supported IDE’s for developing code in this course: PyCharm, VS Code, and REPL”

VS Code is probably the best choice for you because:

  • lightweight and beginner friendly

  • integrates VERY well with Git/GitHub

  • great Python support

  • widely used professionally

  • easier setup than PyCharm for this type of class

  • useful for your AI/cyber work too

What you should install

1. VS Code

VS Code Download

Install the Mac version.

2. Python (if you don’t already have it)

In Terminal run:

Bash
python3 --version

If it shows Python 3.10+ you’re good.

If not, install from:

Python Downloads

3. VS Code Python Extension

After opening VS Code:

  1. Click Extensions icon (left sidebar)

  2. Search:

    Python

  3. Install the one by Microsoft

How you’ll use it for class

Open your repo folder:

Documents/jhu_software_concepts

Then your assignments will live inside:

Module 1
Module 2
Module 3

You’ll code in VS Code, then push with Git.

Your workflow each week

  1. Open VS Code

  2. Open jhu_software_concepts

  3. Work inside the current module folder

  4. Save files

  5. Push to GitHub:

Bash
git add .
git commit -m "Complete Module 2 assignment"
git push

That setup is exactly aligned with how this course is structured.


You said:

i see this

ChatGPT said:

Perfect — that means VS Code downloaded correctly.

Now just drag the Visual Studio Code icon on the left INTO the Applications folder on the right.

After it copies:

  1. Open Applications

  2. Open VS Code

  3. If Mac gives a security popup, click:

    Open

Then I’ll help you:

  • connect your GitHub repo to VS Code

  • open your jhu_software_concepts folder

  • create your first Python file

  • make sure Python is configured correctly in VS Code.


You said:

now what

ChatGPT said:

Now open VS Code.

When it opens:

1. Open your repo folder

At the top menu click:

File → Open Folder

Then navigate to:

Documents → jhu_software_concepts

Click:

Open

2. Trust the folder

VS Code may ask:

Do you trust the authors of the files?

Click:

Yes, I trust the authors

3. Install the Python extension

On the left sidebar:

  1. Click the square Extensions icon

  2. Search:

    Python

  3. Install:

    Python (Microsoft)

4. Create your first Python file

In the left sidebar:

  1. Right click Module 1

  2. Click:

    New File

  3. Name it:

    test.py

Put this inside:

Python
print("Hello World")

Save with:

Command + S

5. Run the file

Top right corner should show a ▶ Run button.

Click it.

At the bottom terminal you should see:

Hello World

Once you get that working, your coding environment is fully ready for the class.


ChatGPT can make mistakes. Check important info.
